elements.sent-2411-001: It can comprehend the intention behind user questions, handle complex 3D scenes with multiple objects, and produce fine-grained explanations with 3D spatial relations among objects, demonstrating strong reasoning and 3D segmentation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-002: The recent development in multimodal learning has greatly advanced the research in 3D scene understanding in various real-world tasks such as embodied AI.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, most existing work shares two typical constraints: 1) they are short of reasoning ability for interaction and interpretation of human intension and 2) they focus on scenarios with single-category objects only which leads to over-simplified textual descriptions due to the negligence of multi-object scenarios and spatial relations among objects.
elements.sent-2411-004: We bridge the research gaps by proposing a 3D reasoning segmentation task for multiple objects in scenes.
elements.sent-2411-005: The task allows producing 3D segmentation masks and detailed textual explanations as enriched by 3D spatial relations among objects.
elements.sent-2411-006: To this end, we create ReasonSeg3D, a large-scale and high-quality benchmark that integrates 3D spatial relations with generated question-answer pairs and 3D segmentation masks.
elements.sent-2411-007: In addition, we design MORE3D, a simple yet effective method that enables multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation with user questions and textual outputs.
elements.sent-2411-008: Extensive experiments show that MORE3D excels in reasoning and segmenting complex multi-object 3D scenes, and the created ReasonSeg3D offers a valuable platform for future exploration of 3D reasoning segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-009: The dataset and code will be released.
elements.sent-2411-010: With the recent advancements in deep learning, 3D scene understanding has become one key component in various real-world tasks such as embodied AI and autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-011: The capabilities of understanding spatial relations among 3D objects in scenes and grasping the intention of human users have become essential for machines for interpreting 3D scenes, interacting with 3D objects in scenes, and achieving various complex and real-world missions while navigating within 3D environments.
elements.sent-2411-012: On the other hand, most existing 3D scene understanding work does not possess reasoning and interpretation abilities for interacting with user textual inputs.
elements.sent-2411-013: For example, several work introduces foundation models such as large language models (LLMs) to empower 3D scene understanding on captioning~\cite{chen2023unit3d,chen2023end,hong20233d , question answering~\cite{azuma2022scanqa, ma2023sqa3d, parelli2023clip, guo2023point, hong20233d , visual grounding~\cite{huang2022multi, zhu20233d, guo2023viewrefer, yang2024llm, chen2024ll3da, hong20233d, kang2024intent3d , and referring~\cite{qian2024x, wu20243dgres, huang2021text, yuan2021instancerefer , but they are short of reasoning capabilities for deducting human intentions.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Recently, several studies attempt to introduce the reasoning ability of LLMs into 3D scene understanding tasks.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, they mostly focus on segmenting single or single-category objects but cannot handle complex scenes with multiple objects of different categories ~\cite{chen2024ll3da, he2024segpoint,huang2024reason3d .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Hence, they cannot understand 3D spatial relations among objects and produce fine-grained textual explanations, hampering their applications in various real-world scenarios that often come with multiple objects of different categories.
elements.sent-2411-017: We propose a multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation task that can produce 3D segmentation masks and textual explanations with rich 3D spatial relations among objects in scenes, given 3D scenes and user questions as inputs.
elements.sent-2411-018: To this end, we create ReasonSeg3D, a large-scale and high-quality benchmark that can evaluate 3D reasoning segmentation with multiple 3D objects and rich spatial relations among them.
elements.sent-2411-019: Different from prior 3D reasoning segmentation benchmarks~\cite{he2024segpoint,huang2024reason3d , ReasonSeg3D expands the scope into multi-object space which is well aligned with real-world tasks that often come with multiple objects.}
elements.sent-2411-020: In addition, ReasonSeg3D integrates 3D spatial information into question-answer pairs, where the 3D spatial relations in textual answers benefit 3D reasoning segmentation clearly.
elements.sent-2411-021: On top of ReasonSeg3D, we design MORE3D, a simple yet effective technique that enables multi-object reasoning segmentation with textual explanations to users’ questions.
elements.sent-2411-022: MORE3D learns object-specific point cloud embeddings from LLM, enabling predicting precise 3D segmentation masks of multiple objects.
elements.sent-2411-023: In addition, it produces detailed textual explanations that capture 3D spatial relations among multiple objects, supporting accurate segmentation and comprehensive reasoning for complex 3D scenes.
elements.sent-2411-024: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:motivation , MORE3D demonstrates strong reasoning capability for comprehending the intention behind user's input questions and complex 3D scenes, producing accurate 3D segmentation and explanatory answers with respect to multiple 3D objects in scenes.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The major contributions of this work can be summarized in three aspects.
elements.sent-2411-026: First, we introduce a new multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation task, together with a large-scale and high-quality benchmark that incorporates 3D spatial relations for effective evaluations of multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-027: Second, we design a reasoning segmentation technique that can handle multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation and produce fine-grained textual explanations with 3D spatial relations among objects in scenes.
elements.sent-2411-028: Third, extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of our proposed multi-object reasoning segmentation technique as well as the validity of our created benchmark on multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-029: Integrating point clouds with natural language processing has widespread applications, drawing increasing interest in language-instructed 3D scene understanding.
elements.sent-2411-030: Existing language-instructed 3D scene understanding methods can be broadly grouped into two categories.
elements.sent-2411-031: The first category focuses on 3D segmentation~\cite{nguyen2023open3dis, yan2024maskclustering, boudjoghra2024open, ding2023pla, yang2024regionplc, huang2023openins3d, liu2023weakly, qian2024x, wu20243dgres, huang2021text, yuan2021instancerefer , such as OpenScene~\cite{peng2023openscene , Openmask3D~\cite{takmaz2023openmask3d , 3D-STMN~\cite{wu20243d , which produce segmentation masks but lack reasoning abilities and textual or conversational output.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: This limits their ability to comprehend the user's intention and interact with the user in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-033: The second category focuses on tasks such as 3D captioning~\cite{chen2023unit3d, chen2023end, hong20233d , 3D question answering~\cite{azuma2022scanqa, ma2023sqa3d, parelli2023clip, guo2023point, hong20233d , and visual grounding~\cite{huang2022multi, zhu20233d, guo2023viewrefer, yang2024llm, chen2024ll3da, hong20233d, kang2024intent3d .}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: They can generate textual outputs like phrases or conversational outputs, but leave the fine-grained segmentation task untouched and have no reasoning ability either.
elements.sent-2411-035: Different from existing methods, the proposed MORE3D predicts textual answers with explanation and accurate segmentation of multiple 3D objects within complex scenes, demonstrating strong reasoning capability in comprehending the intention behind the user's input questions.
elements.sent-2411-036: Reasoning Segmentation is first introduced by LISA~\cite{lai2024lisa to generate segmentation masks from complex, implicit textual queries.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Specifically, LISA integrates LLaVA~\cite{liu2023visual with SAM, enhancing segmentation through the vision-language model's reasoning capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Following LISA, PixelLM~\cite{ren2024pixellm improves pixel-level reasoning segmentation using multimodal models with a lightweight decoder and segmentation codebook, LLM-Seg~\cite{wang2024llm bridges the Segmentation Anything Model and LLMs by selecting mask proposals, and LLaVASeg~\cite{yang2024empowering incorporates query-focused segmentation into large language models where chain-of-thought prompting is adopted to preserve dialogue functions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In addition, VISA~\cite{yan2024visa extends reasoning segmentation to video by combining multimodal language models with a mask decoder, facilitating complex video segmentation from implicit text queries and world knowledge.}
elements.sent-2411-040: In the 3D domain, PARIS3D~\cite{kareem2024paris3d and Reasoning3D~\cite{chen2024reasoning3d focus on part segmentation with explanations for individual 3D objects, leaving 3D segmentation of complex scenes untouched.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Recent studies like SegPoint~\cite{he2024segpoint and Reason3D~\cite{huang2024reason3d integrate LLMs' reasoning ability into 3D segmentation, but they are limited to segmentation for single-category objects and have no textual explanations.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: In contrast, our approach targets multi-object 3D segmentation and provides textual explanations in the output answer, enhancing the model's understanding of 3D spatial relations and offering a more practical solution for real-world complex scenes.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figure/dataset_generation_pipeline.jpg} \caption{ Illustration of the prompt template in our dataset generation. (a) One example prompt template in our dataset generation on 3D multi-object reasoning. (b) With a sample input image to GPT-4o and the corresponding ground-truth segmentation on the top, the two boxes below present one generated question-answer pair where text of different colors highlights different objects. } \label{fig:dataset_generation}
elements.figure-2411-002: \setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt} 0.94{ \begin{tabular}{l|c|cc|cccc} \multirow{2}{*}{Dataset} \multirow{2}{*}{Venue} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{Scale} \multicolumn{4}{c}{Data for Each QA Pair} \# Scene \# QA Pair Single Object Multiple Objects Multiple Categories Explanation Instruct3D~\cite{he2024segpoint} ECCV 2024 280 2,565 \checkmark \checkmark \texttimes \texttimes Reason3D~\cite{huang2024reason3d} arXiv N/A 2,484 \checkmark \texttimes \texttimes \texttimes ReasonSeg3D (Ours) - 1,513 20,113 \checkmark \checkmark \checkmark \checkmark \end{tabular} } \caption{ Comparison of existing 3D scene-level reasoning segmentation datasets. ReasonSeg3D stands out for its large-scale, high-quality data, supporting segmenting multiple 3D objects across multiple categories and offering explanations with 3D spatial relations. } \label{tab:comparison_dataset}
elements.sent-2411-043: Inspired by the remarkable learning ability of Large Language Models (LLMs), recent research has expanded into the visual domain and developed a series of Large Multimodal Models (LMMs)~\cite{alayrac2022flamingo, zhang2023video .}
elements.sent-2411-044: The prevalent approach focuses on aligning visual representations with the linguistic embeddings of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-045: For example, BLIP-2~\cite{li2023blip and mPLUG-OWL~\cite{ye2023mplug encode image features with a visual encoder, integrating them into the LLMs with text embeddings.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: LLaVA~\cite{liu2023visual and MiniGPT4~\cite{zhu2024minigpt align image-text features followed by instruction tuning, and they also explore image retrieval for LLMs.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Recent studies delve into the integration of multimodal LLMs with vision tasks.
elements.sent-2411-048: For example, VisionLLM~\cite{wang2023visionllm provides an interface for vision-centric tasks via instruction tuning though it does not exploit LLMs for complex reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-049: VisionLLM-v2~\cite{wu2024visionllm integrates visual perception, understanding, and generation within a unified framework by using a ``super link" to connect the multimodal large model with task-specific decoders.}
elements.sent-2411-050: DetGPT~\cite{pi2023detgpt introduces multimodal LLMs into open-vocabulary detectors for instruction-based detection tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-051: GPT4RoI~\cite{zhang2023gpt4roi introduces spatial boxes as inputs, training on region-text pairings.}
elements.sent-2411-052: LISA~\cite{lai2024lisa enhances segmentation in multimodal LLMs by introducing a <SEG> token.}
elements.sent-2411-053: These existing studies primarily target downstream tasks in the 2D domain.
elements.sent-2411-054: In contrast, our approach extends into the 3D domain to enable multi-object segmentation of 3D point clouds and provides textual explanations to support reasoning in the segmentation process.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figure/overall_architecture.jpg} \caption{ Overview of our proposed MORE3D: Given an input point cloud, the 3D Encoder first extracts per-point features $F_p$ and projects them into sequential features $F_s$. The sequential features $F_s$, together with the textual input $X_{que}$, are then fed into a multimodal LLM to perform reasoning, producing textual answers $\hat{Y}_{ans}$ with both detailed explanations and descriptions of 3D spatial relationships among multiple objects. Finally, embeddings for multiple <SEG> tokens and the per-point features are passed to the 3D Decoder to produce 3D segmentation masks and classification results. The module marked with a snowflake icon is frozen during training, while those marked with a flame icon are trainable. } \label{fig:overall_architecture}
elements.sent-2411-055: Most existing reasoning segmentation datasets are not suitable for studying the proposed 3D multi-object reasoning segmentation task.
elements.sent-2411-056: Specifically, existing reasoning segmentation datasets have two critical limitations.
elements.sent-2411-057: First, the existing 2D reasoning segmentation datasets~\cite{lai2024lisa, ren2024pixellm, wang2024llm, yan2024visa lack 3D data, making them unsuitable for 3D tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Second, the existing 3D reasoning datasets~\cite{he2024segpoint, huang2024reason3d focus on single-category objects as illustrated in Table~\ref{tab:comparison_dataset , and they contain only a few hundred scenes which are far fewer than standard 3D segmentation datasets~\cite{dai2017scannet, rozenberszki2022language .}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Additionally, these 3D reasoning datasets lack textual explanations with spatial information, hindering them from training MLLMs for better 3D spatial relation understanding.
elements.sent-2411-060: We bridge this gap by proposing a data generation pipeline as well as a new 3D reasoning segmentation dataset ReasonSeg3D, more details to be elaborated in the following subsections.
elements.sent-2411-061: In the proposed ReasonSeg3D, each point cloud $P$ is paired with multiple $\{x_{que , y_{ans , M\}$ triplets, where $x_{que}$ is a question targeting one or more objects in the image, $y_{ans}$ is the textual answer containing the explanation for the reasoning segmentation, and $M$ represents the 3D segmentation masks corresponding to $y_{ans}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The question $x_{que}$ is designed to require world knowledge and reasoning ability to accurately identify and segment multiple objects.
elements.sent-2411-063: For example, instead of directly asking ``Where are the sofa and table?", ReasonSeg3D formulates the question by ``Where would be the most suitable place for reading a book in this layout?".
elements.sent-2411-064: An answer $y_{ans}$ in ReasonSeg3D is constructed to involve multiple objects, and it also include explanations with 3D spatial relations.
elements.sent-2411-065: For example, the $y_{ans}$ to the above question is formulated by ``The corner with the sofa and a small table next to it can serve as a perfect reading nook, providing comfort and a quiet atmosphere.".
elements.sent-2411-066: Several reasoning segmentation datasets~\cite{lai2024lisa, wang2024llm employ LLaVA~\cite{liu2023visual for image captioning and text-only GPT-4 to produce question-answer pairs according to the generated captions.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: However, GPT-4 cannot understand the image content well and its generated question-answer pairs often lack crucial 3D spatial relations that are essential for 3D reasoning segmentation in various real-world tasks.
elements.sent-2411-068: We design a novel data generation pipeline that introduces GPT-4o that possesses superior visual content understanding capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-069: The pipeline allows generating more practical questions and answers by incorporating spatial relations among objects in scenes.
elements.sent-2411-070: Specifically, we employ both scene image and its ground-truth segmentation to enhance GPT-4o's 3D spatial understanding capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-071: The prompt template is structured in two parts: the first part contains basic requirements for the GPT-4o and the second specifies detailed requirements for generating questions and answers with a focus on describing 3D spatial relations among objects.
elements.sent-2411-072: With this template, GPT-4o autonomously selects objects to form question-answer pairs that reflect the scene’s content and spatial layout.
elements.sent-2411-073: Figure~\ref{fig:dataset_generation shows one example of the designed prompt template and one question-answer pair generated by using the template.}
elements.sent-2411-074: ReasonSeg3D comprises 1513 scenes and 20,113 data samples in total, with point clouds sourced from ScanNetv2~\cite{dai2017scannet .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Following \cite{dai2017scannet , we divide ReasonSeg3D into training and validation sets, comprising 1201 and 312 scenes, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-076: In the generated dataset, each scene has 13.3 questions on average.
elements.sent-2411-077: The dataset contains 20 different object categories for segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-078: Multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation takes a single point cloud $P$ and input user questions $X_{que}$ as input, aiming to reason the implicit intention behind $X_{que}$ and produce textual answers $\hat{Y _{ans}$ including explanations and 3D segmentation masks $\hat{M}$ of multiple objects in $P$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Figure~\ref{fig:overall_architecture shows the framework of the proposed MORE3D which enables Multi-Object 3D Reasoning segmentation and provides textual Explanations based on the user's input question.}
elements.sent-2411-080: Given an input point cloud $P$, the 3D Encoder first extracts per-point features $F_p$ and then projects $F_p$ into sequential features $F_s$ that can be processed by LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-081: The sequential features $F_s$ and input text queries are processed by the LLM, generating textual answers $\hat{Y _{ans}$ that include textual explanations $\hat{Y _{text}$ and multi-object <SEG> tokens $\hat{Y _{<SEG>}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The <SEG> tokens $\hat{Y _{<SEG>}$ indicate the request for 3D segmentation masks, and the corresponding object embeddings $F_{seg}$ are extracted from the LLM's embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-083: These object embeddings $F_{seg}$ are further combined with the per-point features $F_p$ within the 3D Decoder by dot product, ensuring that the object-specific information interacts with the overall point cloud features to generate the final 3D segmentation masks $\hat{M}$ and classification predictions.
elements.sent-2411-084: We design an object-specific point embedding extraction approach to achieve the multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-085: The embedding extraction approach enables precise segmentation and reasoning for multiple objects of different categories in complex point clouds, integrating the LLM with a 3D point cloud decoder to generate segmentation masks and textual explanations.
elements.sent-2411-086: The LLM takes point cloud features $F_s$ and text queries $X_{que}$ as input, generating textual answers $\hat{Y _{ans}$, which include textual explanations $\hat{Y _{text}$ and multi-object <SEG> tokens $\hat{Y _{<SEG>}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Specifically, each output textual answer consists of a paragraph of textual explanations accompanied by a set of <SEG> tokens, where the number of <SEG> tokens equals the number of 3D objects indicated in the input text query.
elements.sent-2411-088: In each answer, the 3D object's name is followed by a <SEG> token.
elements.sent-2411-089: For example, the LLM might generate the following answer: ``You can use the spacious sofa <SEG> for seating, positioned near a central table <SEG> for drinks and snacks, while additional chairs <SEG> provide extra seating options for guests." Each <SEG> token indicates a request for a point cloud segmentation mask.
elements.sent-2411-090: After obtaining the multi-object <SEG> tokens $\hat{Y _{<SEG>}$ in the output textual answer $\hat{Y _{ans}$, we extract the point cloud embeddings generated by the multimodal LLM for each <SEG> token and feed them into the 3D Decoder to generate the corresponding segmentation masks.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:object_specific_feature_extraction , each point cloud $P$ is associated with $N$ textual answers $\hat{Y _{ans}$, and we illustrate the process with one textual answer $\hat{y ^{n _{ans}$ for clarity.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The LLM's textual answer includes multiple <SEG> tokens, and we use a multi-seg index list $I_{seg}$ to record the positions of these tokens in the tokenized output.
elements.sent-2411-093: The multi-seg index list $I_{seg}$ is defined by: \begin{equation}I_{seg} = \{id_0, id_1, ..., id_S \},\end{equation} where $S$ denotes the number of <SEG> tokens in the textual answer.
elements.sent-2411-094: The process can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation}F_{seg} = \{f_i, i \in I_{seg} \},\end{equation} where $f_i$ is the $i$-th point cloud embeddings in the LLM’s hidden states.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figure/object_specific_feature_extraction.jpg} \caption{ Extraction of object-specific point cloud embeddings. Each predicted textual answer contains multiple <SEG> tokens, with their positions recorded in a multi-segment index list $I_{seg}$. The LLM embedding corresponding to each <SEG> token is extracted based on the multi-segment index list for obtaining object-specific point cloud embeddings. } \label{fig:object_specific_feature_extraction}
elements.sent-2411-095: For the example in Figure~\ref{fig:object_specific_feature_extraction , the 3rd and 5th hidden embeddings corresponding to the table and chair are extracted, matching the first and second <SEG> tokens in the answer.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Each <SEG> token corresponds to a 3D object in $\hat{y ^{n _{ans}$, allowing us to obtain the point cloud embeddings from the LLM for subsequent segmentation.}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figure/visualization.jpg} \caption{ Segmentation visualization over the ReasonSeg3D validation set. Each case presents a user input question, the corresponding input point cloud, the ground-truth segmentation, and the prediction by the proposed MORE3D. Best viewed in color and zoom-in. } \label{fig:visualization}
elements.sent-2411-097: The LLM-generated textual answers $\hat{Y _{ans}$ contain explanations of the implicit intention of the user questions.}
elements.sent-2411-098: In addition, the answer includes the descriptions of 3D spatial relations of multiple objects which provide useful guidance for identifying these objects.
elements.sent-2411-099: Unlike most existing reasoning-based segmentation methods~\cite{wang2024llm, yan2024visa, he2024segpoint, huang2024reason3d that just output statements like "It is <SEG>." without further explanation, our generated answer integrates detailed explanations about 3D spatial relations to enhance segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-100: The ground truth answers $Y_{ans}$ also incorporate these 3D spatial relations and are used to supervise the generated output textual answers, guiding the model to capture spatial information effectively.
elements.sent-2411-101: The textual answer loss is defined by: \begin{equation}L_{ans} = CE(Y_{ans}, \hat{Y}_{ans}), \label{equ:l_ans}\end{equation} where $CE$ denotes the cross-entropy loss.
elements.sent-2411-102: For point cloud segmentation, we optimize each predicted point cloud segmentation mask as follows: \begin{equation}L_{mask} = BCE(M, \hat{M}) + DICE(M, \hat{M}), \label{equ:l_mask}\end{equation} where $M$ denotes the ground truth segmentation mask, $\hat{M}$ denotes the predicted segmentation mask, $BCE$ denotes the binary cross-entropy loss and $DICE$ denotes the Dice loss~\cite{milletari2016v .}
elements.sent-2411-103: We use the cross-entropy loss to supervise the point cloud classification as follows: \begin{equation}L_{cls} = CE(C, \hat{C}),\end{equation} where $C$ is the ground truth classification label and $\hat{C}$ is the classification prediction.
elements.sent-2411-104: The proposed model is trained end-to-end with a textual answer loss $L_{ans}$ for textual answer generation, a mask loss $L_{mask}$ for point cloud segmentation mask prediction, and a classification loss $L_{cls}$ for point cloud classification.
elements.sent-2411-105: The overall loss function is formulated as follows: \begin{equation}L = L_{ans} + L_{mask} + L_{cls}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-106: Following prior studies on reasoning segmentation~\cite{lai2024lisa, ren2024pixellm, wang2024llm , we adopt two evaluation metrics including cumulative IoU (cIoU) and generalized IoU (gIoU).}
elements.figure-2411-006: [] 1.0{ \setlength{\tabcolsep}{8pt} \begin{tabular}{l|c|cc} Method Venue cIoU gIoU OpenScene~\cite{peng2023openscene} CVPR 23 7.69 9.52 PLA~\cite{ding2023pla} CVPR 23 10.76 10.27 RegionPLC~\cite{yang2024regionplc} CVPR 24 10.82 11.06 MORE3D (Ours) - 30.19 32.01 \end{tabular} } \caption{ Benchmarking on the ReasonSeg3D validation set with evaluation metrics cIoU and gIoU. Best in bold, second underlined. } \label{tab:sota_comparison}
elements.sent-2411-107: We conduct experiments on one NVIDIA V100 GPU and train the framework for 100 epochs with a batch size of 1.
elements.sent-2411-108: We use the Adam~\cite{kingma2015adam optimizer with the initial learning rate of $1 \times 10^{-4}$.}
elements.sent-2411-109: We adopt LLaMA-7B~\cite{touvron2023llama as our multimodal LLM backbone and LoRA~\cite{hu2021lora to perform efficient fine-tuning.}}
elements.sent-2411-110: All experiments are conducted on the proposed ReasonSeg3D dataset.
elements.sent-2411-111: We benchmark MORE3D with state-of-the-art 3D segmentation methods on the ReasonSeg3D validation set.
elements.sent-2411-112: As Table~\ref{tab:sota_comparison shows, our method achieves superior segmentation performance across all evaluation metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Specifically, OpenScene~\cite{peng2023openscene , PLA~\cite{ding2023pla , and RegionPLC~\cite{yang2024regionplc are designed to comprehend input words or phrases based on CLIP-based language models.}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: They lack the ability to reason user intention from input questions and to correctly generate answers targeting at corresponding multiple objects.
elements.sent-2411-115: Consequently, the predicted 3D segmentation masks by these methods cannot be aligned correctly with ground-truth 3D segmentation masks, resulting in low cIoU and gIoU.
elements.sent-2411-116: In contrast, MORE3D can accurately reason the implicit intention of user questions, predicting 3D segmentation masks that are more consistent with the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-117: Figure~\ref{fig:visualization presents qualitative segmentation by the proposed MORE3D with two examples from the ReasonSeg3D validation set.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Each example demonstrates the user question, input point cloud, ground-truth segmentation, and the 3D segmentation masks that are predicted by MORE3D.
elements.sent-2411-119: We can observe that MORE3D could accurately predict the 3D segmentation masks that are highly aligned with the ground-truth segmentation masks.
elements.sent-2411-120: Specifically, in the example at the top, despite many chairs closely surrounding the central long table, MORE3D can correctly distinguish and segment them.
elements.sent-2411-121: For the example at the bottom, despite the similar appearance and size of tables and chairs, MORE3D can distinguish and segment them precisely.
elements.sent-2411-122: The visualization indicates that MORE3D can comprehend the implicit intention behind the user questions and segment multiple objects accurately.
elements.sent-2411-123: We conduct extensive ablation studies on the ReasonSeg3D validation set to evaluate our designs.
elements.sent-2411-124: Specifically, we examine MORE3D from the aspect of the approach for object-specific point cloud embeddings extraction, the loss design, the point cloud decoding approach, as well as the way of prediction.
elements.sent-2411-125: We examine the effectiveness of the designed multi-seg index list $I_{seg}$ for the object-specific point cloud embeddings extraction in MORE3D.
elements.sent-2411-126: As Table~\ref{tab:object_specific_feature_extraction shows, using the multi-seg index list $I_{seg}$ can achieve better performance than adopting the random selection approach.}
elements.sent-2411-127: This is largely because the LLM's embeddings corresponding to multiple objects are extracted precisely with the help of $I_{seg}$.
elements.sent-2411-128: As a comparison, the random selection could extract embeddings unrelated to target objects like ``table" or ``chairs", and it could also extract embeddings belonging to irrelevant non-object words, such as ``around" and ``suitable".
elements.sent-2411-129: This explains why using the multi-seg index list $I_{seg}$ is more effective for extracting object-specific point cloud embeddings corresponding to multiple target objects.
elements.figure-2411-007: [] \setlength{\tabcolsep}{15pt} 1.0{ \begin{tabular}{c|c|cc} Index Approach cIoU gIoU 1 Random 20.30 21.23 2 with $I_{seg}$ 30.19 32.01 \end{tabular} } \caption{ Ablation study on the approach of object-specific point cloud embeddings extraction. The best results are in bold. } \label{tab:object_specific_feature_extraction}
elements.figure-2411-008: [] \setlength{\tabcolsep}{11pt} 1.0{ \begin{tabular}{c|cc|cc} Index $L_{ans}$ $L_{mask}$ cIoU gIoU 1 13.72 14.73 2 \checkmark 15.23 16.14 3 \checkmark 23.30 28.91 4 \checkmark \checkmark 30.19 32.01 \end{tabular} } \caption{ Ablation study of the loss functions on the ReasonSeg3D validation set. $L_{ans}$ and $L_{mask}$ refer to the textual answer loss and the point cloud segmentation mask loss, respectively. The best results are in bold. } \label{tab:loss_ablation}
elements.sent-2411-130: We examine the impact of the textual answer loss $L_{ans}$ and the mask loss $L_{mask}$ in Equations~\ref{equ:l_ans and~\ref{equ:l_mask , where $L_{ans}$ supervises the textual answers output by the multimodal LLM and $L_{mask}$ supervises the 3D point cloud segmentation prediction.}}
elements.sent-2411-131: As Table~\ref{tab:loss_ablation shows, without $L_{ans}$ and $L_{mask}$, the performance drops greatly due to the lack of supervision from both ground-truth textual answers and segmentation masks, which hinders the model from generating appropriate answers and accurate 3D segmentation masks.}
elements.sent-2411-132: When either $L_{ans}$ or $L_{mask}$ is used, the performance improves consistently due to partial supervision from the ground-truth textual answers or the segmentation masks.
elements.sent-2411-133: When both $L_{ans}$ and $L_{mask}$ are employed, the reasoning ability for generating answers and the capability of predicting 3D segmentation masks of MORE3D are both improved by large margins, demonstrating the effectiveness and synergies of the two designed losses.
elements.sent-2411-134: We examine how different point cloud decoding operations affect 3D reasoning segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-135: Specifically, we evaluate three approaches of point cloud decoding operations that integrate per-point features and object embeddings to predict the 3D segmentation masks.
elements.sent-2411-136: As Table~\ref{tab:dot_product shows, the Addition and the Concatenation operations achieve lower performance compared with the Dot Product operation since they either add or concatenate the embeddings of each object with the per-point features of the global scene, introducing abundant features of irrelevant objects and leading to inferior performance.}
elements.sent-2411-137: Differently, the Dot Product operation selectively extracts only the relevant features from the per-point features of the global scene by identifying those highly correlated with the object embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-138: This leads to better performance thanks to its extracted object-specific features.
elements.figure-2411-009: [] \setlength{\tabcolsep}{12pt} 1.0{ \begin{tabular}{c|c|cc} Index Operation cIoU gIoU 1 Addition 28.26 31.01 2 Concatenation 29.42 31.17 3 Dot Product 30.19 32.01 \end{tabular} } \caption{ Ablation study on the approach of point cloud decoding operation in the 3D Decoder. The best results are in bold. } \label{tab:dot_product}
elements.figure-2411-010: [] \setlength{\tabcolsep}{14pt} 1.0{ \begin{tabular}{c|c|cc} Index Manner cIoU gIoU 1 Unified 29.06 30.43 2 Separated 30.19 32.01 \end{tabular} } \caption{ Ablation study on different approaches of prediction in the 3D Decoder. The best results are in bold. } \label{tab:prediction_manner}
elements.sent-2411-139: We examine the effectiveness of different prediction approaches including unified and separated approaches for the prediction of 3D segmentation masks and classification.
elements.sent-2411-141: For the unified approach, the 3D segmentation masks and classification results are predicted by a single instead of two separate network branches.
elements.sent-2411-142: This results in lower performance due to the increased learning burden when a single network branch must learn to perform segmentation and classification simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-143: In contrast, the separate approach as illustrated in the bottom right of Figure~\ref{fig:overall_architecture assigns each task to an independent network branch to learn.}
elements.sent-2411-144: This allow each branch to focus on its respective task which lowers the learning burden and improves performance clearly.
elements.sent-2411-145: This paper presents a novel multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation task, producing both 3D segmentation masks and textual explanations with rich 3D spatial relations among multiple objects within complex 3D scenes.
elements.sent-2411-146: To this end, we develop ReasonSeg3D, a large-scale benchmark featuring rich 3D spatial relations integrated into question-answer pairs, designed to evaluate multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation effectively.
elements.sent-2411-147: On top of this, we propose MORE3D, a technique for multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation with textual explanations, demonstrating strong reasoning abilities in response to user questions.
elements.sent-2411-148: Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of MORE3D.
elements.sent-2411-149: Future work will focus on generalizing our work to diverse and challenging 3D environments, such as outdoor scenes, to expand the scope of applications.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-150: It can comprehend the intention behind user questions, handle complex 3D scenes with multiple objects, and produce fine-grained explanations with 3D spatial relations among objects, demonstrating strong reasoning and 3D segmentation capabilities.. \end{center ].}
elements.sent-2411-151: To this end, we create ReasonSeg3D, a large-scale and high-quality benchmark that integrates 3D segmentation masks and 3D spatial relations with generated question-answer pairs.
elements.sent-2411-152: In addition, we design MORE3D, a novel 3D reasoning network that works with queries of multiple objects and tailored 3D scene understanding designs.
elements.sent-2411-153: MORE3D learns detailed explanations on 3D relations and employs them to capture spatial information of objects and reason textual outputs.
elements.sent-2411-154: However, they mostly focus on segmenting single or single-category objects but cannot handle complex scenes with multiple objects of different categories~\cite{chen2024ll3da, he2024segpoint,huang2024reason3d .}
elements.sent-2411-155: Specifically, MORE3D extracts object-specific point cloud embedding for precise prediction of multi-category objects in complex 3D scenes.
elements.sent-2411-156: It incorporates textual explanations with detailed 3D spatial relations of multiple objects in answers, guiding the model toward a comprehensive understanding of 3D scenes.
elements.sent-2411-157: The generated textual answers support accurate segmentation and comprehensive reasoning for complex 3D scenes.
elements.sent-2411-158: On top of that, we conduct human verification to ensure the quality of the generated question-answer pairs.
elements.sent-2411-159: We design an object-specific point cloud embedding extraction approach to achieve the multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figure/visualization.jpg} \caption{ Segmentation visualization over the ReasonSeg3D validation set. Each case presents a user input question, the corresponding input point cloud, the ground-truth segmentation, and the prediction by the proposed MORE3D. Best viewed in color and zoom-in. \textcolor[RGB]{185,186,32}{Green} indicates chairs, and \textcolor[RGB]{255,152,151}{pink} indicates tables. Best viewed in color. } \label{fig:visualization}
elements.figure-2411-012: [] 1.0{ \setlength{\tabcolsep}{8pt} \begin{tabular}{l|c|cc} Method Venue cIoU gIoU \rowcolor{gray!20} \multicolumn{4}{l}{3D Segmentation Methods} OpenScene~\cite{peng2023openscene} CVPR 23 7.69 9.52 PLA~\cite{ding2023pla} CVPR 23 10.76 10.27 RegionPLC~\cite{yang2024regionplc} CVPR 24 10.82 11.06 3D-STMN~\cite{wu20243d} AAAI 24 19.37 20.70 RefMask3D~\cite{he2024refmask3d} MM 24 20.53 22.81 MDIN~\cite{wu20243dgres} MM 24 23.04 23.49 \rowcolor{gray!20} \multicolumn{4}{l}{3D Vision-Language Methods} 3D-LLM~\cite{hong20233d} NeurIPS 23 21.36 22.49 3D-VisTA~\cite{zhu20233d} ICCV 23 23.95 24.77 MORE3D (Ours) - 30.19 32.01 \end{tabular} } \caption{ Benchmarking on the ReasonSeg3D validation set with evaluation metrics cIoU and gIoU. Best in bold, second underlined. } \label{tab:sota_comparison}
elements.sent-2411-160: We benchmark MORE3D with state-of-the-art 3D segmentation and vision-language methods on the ReasonSeg3D validation set.
elements.sent-2411-161: As Table~\ref{tab:sota_comparison shows, our method achieves superior reasoning segmentation performance across all evaluation metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-162: Moreover, 3D-STMN~\cite{wu20243d and RefMask3D~\cite{he2024refmask3d are tailored for single-object segmentation and therefore cannot handle multiple objects, while MDIN~\cite{wu20243dgres exhibits weaker reasoning ability for interpreting implicit human intention.}}}
elements.sent-2411-163: Additionally, 3D-LLM~\cite{hong20233d and 3D-VisTA~\cite{zhu20233d , designed for general 3D tasks, are re-implemented on our proposed 3D reasoning segmentation task for comparison.}}
elements.sent-2411-164: Besides the dot product for refined queries and features, we explore addition and concatenation to investigate whether richer point cloud information improves training.
elements.sent-2411-165: However, most existing studies are facing two common challenges: 1) they are short of reasoning ability for interaction and interpretation of human intentions and 2) they focus on scenarios with single-category objects and over-simplified textual descriptions and neglect multi-object scenarios with complicated spatial relations among objects.
elements.sent-2411-166: We address the above challenges by proposing a 3D reasoning segmentation task for reasoning segmentation with multiple objects in scenes.
elements.sent-2411-167: In addition, we design MORE3D, a novel 3D reasoning network that works with queries of multiple objects and is tailored for 3D scene understanding.
elements.sent-2411-168: Extensive experiments show that MORE3D excels in reasoning and segmenting complex multi-object 3D scenes.
elements.sent-2411-169: In addition, the created ReasonSeg3D offers a valuable platform for future exploration of 3D reasoning segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-170: The data and code will be released.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=0.93\linewidth]{figure/motivation.jpg} \caption{ The proposed MORE3D enables multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation. It can comprehend the intention behind user questions or instructions, handle complex 3D scenes with multiple objects of different categories, and produce fine-grained explanations with 3D spatial relations among objects, demonstrating strong reasoning and 3D segmentation capabilities. } \label{fig:motivation}
elements.sent-2411-171: For example, several studies introduce foundation models such as large language models (LLMs) to empower 3D scene understanding on captioning~\cite{chen2023unit3d,chen2023end,hong20233d , question answering~\cite{azuma2022scanqa, ma2023sqa3d, parelli2023clip, guo2023point, hong20233d , visual grounding~\cite{huang2022multi, zhu20233d, guo2023viewrefer, yang2024llm, chen2024ll3da, hong20233d, kang2024intent3d , and referring~\cite{qian2024x, wu20243dgres, huang2021text, yuan2021instancerefer , but they are short of reasoning capabilities for deducing human intentions.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: Several recent studies attempt to introduce the reasoning ability of LLMs into 3D scene understanding tasks, but most of them focus on segmenting single or single-category objects and cannot handle complex scenes with multiple objects of different categories~\cite{chen2024ll3da, he2024segpoint,huang2025reason3d .}
elements.sent-2411-173: As a result, they cannot understand 3D spatial relations among objects and produce fine-grained textual explanations, hampering their applications in various real-world scenarios that often come with multiple objects of different categories.
elements.sent-2411-174: The task consists of two key components.
elements.sent-2411-175: The first is ReasonSeg3D, a large-scale and high-quality benchmark that can evaluate 3D reasoning segmentation with multiple 3D objects and rich spatial relations among them.
elements.sent-2411-176: Different from existing 3D reasoning segmentation benchmarks~\cite{he2024segpoint,huang2025reason3d , ReasonSeg3D expands the scope into multi-object space which is well aligned with real-world tasks that often come with multiple objects.}
elements.sent-2411-177: The second is MORE3D, a simple yet effective technique that enables multi-object reasoning segmentation with textual explanations to users’ questions.
elements.sent-2411-178: MORE3D extracts object-specific point cloud embedding for precise prediction of multi-category objects in complex 3D scenes.
elements.sent-2411-179: The first category focuses on 3D segmentation~\cite{nguyen2023open3dis, yan2024maskclustering, boudjoghra2024open, ding2023pla, yang2024regionplc, huang2023openins3d, liu2023weakly, qian2024x, wu20243dgres, huang2021text, yuan2021instancerefer, peng2023openscene, takmaz2023openmask3d, wu20243d, wu2024rg , such as OpenScene~\cite{peng2023openscene , Openmask3D~\cite{takmaz2023openmask3d , 3D-STMN~\cite{wu20243d , RG-SAN~\cite{wu2024rg , which produce segmentation masks but lack reasoning abilities and textual or conversational output.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: The second category focuses on tasks such as 3D captioning~\cite{chen2023unit3d, chen2023end, hong20233d , 3D question answering~\cite{azuma2022scanqa, ma2023sqa3d, parelli2023clip, jiang2025exploring, guo2023point, hong20233d , and visual grounding~\cite{huang2022multi, zhu20233d, guo2023viewrefer, yang2024llm, chen2024ll3da, hong20233d, kang2024intent3d, zhang2023multi3drefer, zhang2024task, zhu2024scanreason .}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Recent studies like SegPoint~\cite{he2024segpoint and Reason3D~\cite{huang2025reason3d integrate LLMs' reasoning ability into 3D segmentation, but they are limited to segmentation for single-category objects and have no textual explanations.}}
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=0.93\linewidth]{figure/dataset_generation_pipeline.jpg} \caption{ Illustration of the prompt template in our dataset generation. (a) One example prompt template in our dataset generation for 3D multi-object reasoning. (b) With a sample input image to GPT-4o and the corresponding ground-truth segmentation on the top, the two boxes below present one generated question-answer pair where the text of different colors highlights different objects. } \label{fig:dataset_generation}
elements.sent-2411-182: Second, the existing 3D reasoning datasets~\cite{he2024segpoint, huang2025reason3d focus on single-category objects as illustrated in Table~\ref{tab:comparison_dataset , and they contain only a few hundred scenes which are far fewer than standard 3D segmentation datasets~\cite{dai2017scannet, rozenberszki2022language .}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: We bridge this gap by proposing a data generation pipeline as well as a new 3D reasoning segmentation dataset ReasonSeg3D, with more details to be elaborated in the following subsections.
elements.figure-2411-015: \caption{ Comparison of existing 3D scene-level reasoning segmentation datasets. ReasonSeg3D stands out for its large-scale, high-quality data, supporting segmenting multiple 3D objects across multiple categories and offering explanations with 3D spatial relations. } \setlength{\tabcolsep}{6pt} 0.7{ \begin{tabular}{l|c|cc|cccc} \multirow{2}{*}{Dataset} \multirow{2}{*}{Venue} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{Scale} \multicolumn{4}{c}{Data for Each QA Pair} \# Scene \# QA Pair Single Object Multiple Objects Multiple Categories Explanation Instruct3D~\cite{he2024segpoint} ECCV 2024 280 2,565 \checkmark \checkmark \texttimes \texttimes Reason3D~\cite{huang2025reason3d} arXiv N/A 2,484 \checkmark \texttimes \texttimes \texttimes ReasonSeg3D (Ours) - 1,513 20,113 \checkmark \checkmark \checkmark \checkmark \end{tabular} } \label{tab:comparison_dataset}
elements.sent-2411-184: An answer $y_{ans}$ in ReasonSeg3D is constructed to involve multiple objects, and it also includes explanations with 3D spatial relations.
elements.sent-2411-185: Several reasoning segmentation datasets~\cite{lai2024lisa, wang2024llm employ LLaVA~\cite{liu2023visual for image captioning and text-only GPT-4 to produce question-answer pairs according to generated captions.}}
elements.sent-2411-186: We design a novel data generation pipeline that introduces GPT-4o which possesses superior visual content understanding capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-187: The pipeline allows the generation of more practical questions and answers by incorporating spatial relations among objects in scenes.
elements.sent-2411-188: Specifically, we employ scene image and ground-truth segmentation to enhance GPT-4o's 3D spatial understanding capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-189: Our dataset focuses on indoor scenes, which are critical for a wide range of real-world embodied applications, such as household robotics and augmented reality, where accurate reasoning and perception in complex, cluttered environments are essential.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=0.93\linewidth]{figure/overall_architecture.jpg} \caption{ Overview of our proposed MORE3D: Given an input point cloud, the 3D Encoder first extracts per-point features $F_p$ and projects them into sequential features $F_s$. The sequential features $F_s$, together with the textual input $X_{que}$, are then fed into a multimodal LLM to perform reasoning, obtaining LLM embeddings and then producing textual answers $\hat{Y}_{ans}$ with both detailed explanations and descriptions of 3D spatial relations among multiple objects. Finally, after object-specific embedding extraction, object embeddings for multiple <SEG> tokens $F_{seg}$ and the per-point features $F_p$ are passed to the 3D Decoder to produce 3D segmentation masks. The module marked with a snowflake icon is frozen during training, while those marked with a flame icon are trainable. } \label{fig:overall_architecture}
elements.sent-2411-190: The sequential features $F_s$ and input text queries are processed by the LLM, obtaining LLM embeddings and then generating textual answers $\hat{Y _{ans}$ that include textual explanations $\hat{Y _{text}$ and multi-object <SEG> tokens $\hat{Y _{<SEG>}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: The <SEG> tokens $\hat{Y _{<SEG>}$ indicate the request for 3D segmentation masks, and the corresponding object embeddings $F_{seg}$ are extracted from the LLM embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-192: These object embeddings $F_{seg}$ are further combined with per-point features $F_p$ within 3D Decoder by dot product, ensuring that the object-specific information interacts with the overall point cloud features to generate the final 3D segmentation masks $\hat{M}$ and classification predictions.
elements.sent-2411-193: Different from prior studies on 2D reasoning segmentation~\cite{lai2024lisa, ren2024pixellm, wang2024llm , MORE3D is tailored for 3D tasks and features a 3D-specific encoder-decoder for capturing complex 3D information.}
elements.sent-2411-194: In addition, MORE3D extracts object-specific point cloud embeddings to handle multiple objects in 3D scenes, resolving unique challenges in 3D reasoning segmentation effectively.
elements.sent-2411-195: Further, MORE3D generates textual explanations that incorporate 3D spatial relations among objects, enabling explainable and spatially grounded reasoning of 3D scenes.
elements.sent-2411-196: We design an object-specific embedding extraction approach to achieve multi-object 3D reasoning segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-197: The multi-seg index list $I_{seg}$ is defined by: \begin{equation}I_{seg} = \{id_0, id_1, ..., id_S \},\end{equation} where $S$ denotes the number of <SEG> tokens in the textual answer, and $id$ denotes indices of <SEG> tokens.
elements.sent-2411-198: The process can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation}F_{seg} = \{f_i, i \in I_{seg} \},\end{equation} where $f_i$ is the $i$-th point cloud embedding in the LLM’s hidden states.
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=0.93\linewidth]{figure/object_specific_feature_extraction.jpg} \caption{ Extraction of object-specific point cloud embeddings. Each predicted textual answer contains multiple <SEG> tokens, with their positions recorded in a multi-segment index list $I_{seg}$. The LLM embedding corresponding to each <SEG> token is extracted based on the multi-segment index list for obtaining object-specific point cloud embeddings. } \label{fig:object_specific_feature_extraction}
elements.sent-2411-199: The LLM-generated textual answers $\hat{Y _{ans}$ contain explanations of the implicit intention of the user's questions.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Unlike most existing reasoning-based segmentation methods~\cite{wang2024llm, yan2024visa, he2024segpoint, huang2025reason3d that just output statements like "It is <SEG>" without further explanation, our generated answer integrates detailed explanations about 3D spatial relations to enhance segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-201: All three loss terms are assigned equal weights, and the overall loss function is formulated as follows: \begin{equation}L = L_{ans} + L_{mask} + L_{cls}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-202: We conduct experiments on one NVIDIA V100 GPU (32 GB memory) and train the network for 100 epochs with a batch size of 1, with a total training time of around 1 day.
elements.sent-2411-203: All experiments are conducted on the proposed ReasonSeg3D dataset.. \renewcommand\arraystretch{1.0.}

elements.sent-2411-205: All compared methods are trained and evaluated on the same ReasonSeg3D to ensure a fair comparison.
elements.sent-2411-206: They lack the ability to reason user intention from input questions and to correctly generate answers targeting multiple corresponding objects.
elements.sent-2411-207: Furthermore, Reason3D~\cite{huang2025reason3d , as a 3D reasoning segmentation approach, is limited to single-object settings and struggles with multi-object reasoning segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Specifically, in the top example, despite many chairs closely surrounding the central long table, MORE3D can correctly distinguish and segment them.
elements.sent-2411-209: In the bottom example, despite the similar appearance and size of tables and chairs, MORE3D can distinguish and segment them precisely.

elements.sent-2411-213: For the unified approach, the 3D segmentation masks and classification results are predicted by a single network instead of two separate network branches.

It can comprehend the intention behind user questions or instructions, handle complex 3D scenes with multiple objects of different categories, and produce fine-grained explanations with 3D spatial relations among objects, demonstrating strong reasoning and 3D segmentation capabilities. } \label{fig:motivation}

elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figure/overall_architecture.jpg} \caption{ Overview of our proposed MORE3D: Given an input point cloud, the 3D Encoder first extracts per-point features $F_p$ and projects them into sequential features $F_s$. elements.sent-2411-219: On top of that, we conduct human verification to eliminate errors, such as incorrect object references and spatial‑relation errors, from the generated question–answer pairs.
elements.sent-2411-220: Since GPT-4o demonstrates excellent generation quality, only about 3\% of the generated question–answer pairs require manual correction.
elements.sent-2411-221: Samples that maximize diversity in both target objects and spatial‑relation descriptions are then selected, thereby avoiding bias and ensuring high‑quality, varied question–answer pairs.
elements.sent-2411-222: ReasonSeg3D comprises 1,513 scenes and 20,113 data samples in total, with point clouds sourced from ScanNetv2~\cite{dai2017scannet .}
elements.sent-2411-223: Following \cite{dai2017scannet , we divide ReasonSeg3D into training and validation sets, comprising 1,201 and 312 scenes, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-224: In ReasonSeg3D, each scene has 13.3 questions on average, with 20 different object categories for segmentation.
elements.figure-2411-024: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figure/object_specific_feature_extraction.jpg} \caption{ Extraction of object-specific point cloud embeddings. Each predicted textual answer contains multiple <SEG> tokens, with their positions recorded in a multi-segment index list $I_{seg}$. The LLM embedding corresponding to each <SEG> token is extracted based on the multi-segment index list for obtaining object-specific point cloud embeddings. } \label{fig:object_specific_feature_extraction}
elements.sent-2411-225: Given an input point cloud $P$, the 3D Encoder first groups and extracts per-point features $F_p$ and then projects $F_p$ into sequential features $F_s$ that can be processed by LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-226: Different from prior studies on 2D reasoning segmentation~\cite{lai2024lisa, ren2024pixellm, wang2024llm relying solely on 2D representations, which cannot effectively capture 3D geometric information or spatial relations in complex 3D scenes, MORE3D is tailored for 3D tasks and features a 3D-specific encoder-decoder for capturing complex 3D information.}
elements.sent-2411-227: This approach enables precise segmentation and reasoning for multiple objects of different categories in complex point clouds, integrating the LLM with a 3D point cloud decoder to generate segmentation masks and textual explanations.
elements.sent-2411-228: Each <SEG> token corresponds to a 3D object in $\hat{y ^{n _{ans}$, allowing us to obtain the point cloud embeddings from the LLM for subsequent segmentation.. \renewcommand\arraystretch{1.0.}}}

elements.figure-2411-026: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figure/visualization.jpg} \caption{ Segmentation visualization over the ReasonSeg3D validation set. Each case presents a user input question, the corresponding input point cloud, the ground-truth segmentation, and the prediction by the proposed MORE3D. Green indicates chairs, and pink indicates tables. Underlined text denotes 3D spatial relations. Best viewed in color and zoom-in. } \label{fig:visualization}

elements.sent-2411-229: All compared methods are trained and evaluated on ReasonSeg3D to ensure a fair comparison.
elements.sent-2411-230: As Table~\ref{tab:sota_comparison shows, our method achieves superior performance across all evaluation metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-231: Specifically, we examine MORE3D with the approach for the way of prediction, and the loss design.
elements.sent-2411-232: This results in lower performance due to the increased learning burden when a single network must learn to perform segmentation and classification simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-233: In contrast, the separate approach as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:overall_architecture assigns each task to an independent network branch to learn.}
elements.sent-2411-234: This allows each branch to focus on its respective task, which lowers the learning burden and improves performance clearly.
elements.sent-2411-235: Future work will focus on exploring our work in more challenging 3D environments to improve applicability in real-world scenarios.
